source_file: The corporate social credit system in China and its transnational impact.txt
text:
as the CEOs of a company to the political aims of the CCP. Up to now, the
eﬀorts of the CCP to harvest economic inﬂuence and transform it into pol-
itical inﬂuence had to be communicated rather directly and could thus spark
discussion and public resistance in the respective countries. With the CSCS
this is more diﬃcult as, seemingly, the algorithm determines the regulatory
outcome. The aspect of co-optation highlights the importance of the human
aspect, the role of the individual, once more. Key employees of big corporate
companies are connected across borders and have private and professional
aspirations, fears, economic dependencies and interests. Their personal sat-
isfaction is partly reliant on their social and economic standing.118 The
CSCS nudges individuals with the promise of increased beneﬁts and
wealth and threatens them with the exclusion from economic and social
life.119
    It is an openly communicated ambition of the Chinese government to
transcend the Chinese market and use the scoring system as a soft power
instrument for gaining international inﬂuence.120 The CSCS might result
in ‘symbolic change’,121 thus change of law in other legal systems. Some
states show interest in the credit system122 and see it as an example worth
imitating. The threshold for integrating some of the system’s norms and
values into national legislation and practice might be relatively low if one
or several means of inﬂuence apply: coercion, reciprocity, persuasion and
acculturation.123 The CSCS could, for example, enter the national sphere
and create symbolic change due to economic dependence, the perception
of legitimacy of embedded norms and values or the resonance with a local
(elite) identity, nepotism or an authoritarian culture of governance.
Another reason for symbolic change might be considerations of eﬀectiveness
or speedy economic development (China being perceived by some as a prime
example for it). Yet, the perspective of transnational law as a method allows
to incorporate other forms of impact such as a change in institutional and
social practice and individual behaviour, thus practical change.124 The EU
